label: Valentinian Gnosticism

scope.school: Valentinus and the Valentinian school (including Ptolemy's system)
scope.period: 2nd century CE — 'Valentinus (ca. 100–175 CE) spent his formative years in Alexandria... Valentinus later went to Rome, where he began his public teaching career'
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.

Reconstruction depends heavily on hostile heresiological reports (Irenaeus, Hippolytus, and others) alongside the Nag Hammadi texts; the IEP entry notes these Church Fathers 'have preserved for us accounts and occasionally some original documents', writing 'almost always in conflict with' the Gnostics.

The very category 'Gnosticism' is contested: Michael Allen Williams's Rethinking 'Gnosticism' argues 'the extreme diversity underlying the texts... casts doubt on the existence of anything like a Gnostic religion'.
Single-encyclopedia draft: claims rest on the IEP Gnosticism entry; the dedicated IEP Valentinus page was unavailable (404) at access time.
No claims are recorded for constituents, time, or epistemic_access as separate primitives beyond the gnosis doctrine. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Beyond the cosmos stands 'the supreme God, the One beyond Being' at the apex of 'the Plêrôma or “Fullness”' of divine aeons.

The material world originates in a divine fault: 'the material cosmos is the result of a primordial error on the part of a supra-cosmic, supremely divine being' — Sophia's 'reckless desire to know the transcendent God' which 'leads to the hypostatization of her desire in the form of a semi-divine and essentially ignorant creature known as the Demiurge'.

The world's maker is not the true God: 'the Demiurge (Greek: dêmiourgos, “craftsman”), or Ialdabaoth, who is responsible for the formation of the material cosmos... hubristically declares himself the only existing God'.
Divine fragments are trapped in matter: 'the human “sparks” (which fell from Sophia)' await release from a cosmos built on error and ruled by an ignorant power.

Humanity is stratified by nature in Ptolemy's Valentinian system: 'three classes of human beings come into existence... the “material” (hulikos), the “animate” (psukhikos), and the “spiritual” (pneumatikos)'.
claims.notes: The tripartition is reported through heresiological sources whose hostile framing the entry itself acknowledges; how deterministic the classes were is debated, and the category 'Gnosticism' itself is contested (Williams 1996).

claims.claim: Salvation comes through knowledge rather than faith alone: 'the “spiritual” humans, the Gnostics, who need no faith, since they have actual knowledge (gnôsis) of intellectual reality'.
The end is restoration to the Fullness: Christ 'descends to the material realm for the purpose of negating all passion, and raising the innocent human “sparks” back up to the Pleroma'.

The true self is the divine spark, not the material person; the spiritual element in humans derives from Sophia and belongs ultimately to the Pleroma, as with 'the human “sparks” (which fell from Sophia)'.

distinguishing_claims: The material cosmos results from a pre-cosmic divine error (Sophia's fall)
The world's creator (Demiurge) is ignorant and distinct from the true transcendent God
Humans contain divine sparks whose salvation is release from matter
Salvation comes through gnosis — knowledge, not faith or works alone
common_confusions: The Gnostic Demiurge is a devalued inversion of the Timaeus craftsman: ignorant and hubristic rather than good and rational.
Valentinianism is anti-cosmic where classical theism affirms creation's goodness; the two cards contradict on the value of the material world.
Valentinus 'was certainly the most overtly Christian of the Gnostic philosophers of his era' — Valentinianism is a Christian movement, not a separate Eastern religion.
'Gnosticism' as a unified category is a modern construct under scholarly challenge.

sources.title: Gnosticism (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Edward Moore
sources.passage: the supreme God, the One beyond Being (at the apex of) the Plêrôma or 'Fullness.' [...] a reckless desire to know the transcendent God, leads to the hypostatization of her desire in the form of a semi-divine and essentially ignorant creature known as the Demiurge. [...] The Demiurge (Greek: dêmiourgos, 'craftsman'), or Ialdabaoth, who is responsible for the formation of the material cosmos... hubristically declares himself the only existing God. [...] Three classes of human beings come into existence... the 'material' (hulikos), the 'animate' (psukhikos), and the 'spiritual' (pneumatikos). [...] [Christ] descends to the material realm for the purpose of negating all passion, and raising the innocent human 'sparks' back up to the Pleroma. [...] Valentinus was certainly the most overtly Christian of the Gnostic philosophers of his era. [...] The great Christian teacher and philosopher Valentinus (ca. 100–175 CE) spent his formative years in Alexandria... Valentinus later went to Rome, where he began his public teaching career. [...] Michael Allen Williams, in 1996, published a book entitled Rethinking 'Gnosticism': An Argument For Dismantling A Dubious Category. [...] the extreme diversity underlying the texts that many scholars have lumped together under the catch-all phrase of 'Gnosticism,' casts doubt on the existence of anything like a Gnostic religion.
